summary:
Minocycline is specifically associated with flagellate pigmentary changes due to its ability to induce wavy or filiform hyperpigmentation in skin and nail beds. This pattern distinguishes it from other medications that may cause similar-looking but different types of pigmentation, making it uniquely tied to a flagellate appearance clinically. 

The summary above contains an error — please correct only what needs correction based strictly upon the provided information:

- The final sentence incorrectly states “making it uniquely tied” when this claim isn't supported by any document; there’s insufficient basis to assert uniqueness beyond association.

Corrected Summary: Minocycline is specifically associated with flagellate pigmentary changes due to its ability to induce wavy or filiform hyperpigmentation in skin and nail beds. Other agents can also produce pigmented lesions, though not typically described as having a flagellate morphology—thus distinguishing minocylline among those causing such specific findings.

Final Answer: Corrected Summary
Minocycline is specifically associated with flagellate pigmentary changes due to its ability to induce wavy or filiform hyperpigmination in skin and nail beds. Other agents can also produce pigmented lesions, though not typically described as having a flagellate morphology—thus distinguishing minocycline among those causing such specific findings.
